1.tldr:
Cope Mercer takes the stage to accept a trophy for being the last human CEO in the consumer Fortune 100, and he's magnificent about it. He preaches the gospel of the "human premium" — the irreplaceable soul only a person can bring to a boardroom — while running on zero sleep and whatever he snorted in the bathroom ten minutes ago. His wife Margaux's seat is empty, and he decides not to notice.

After the applause dies, his aide Sun corners him with the ugly news: Tidewater is coming for the takeover, and they've already named his replacement — a machine. She needs an answer by Monday, and he waves her off like she's a fly at a picnic. He'd rather brood alone in his office than admit the ground is moving under him.

So the great man ends his triumphant night at an all-night Waffle House, nursing coffee under fluorescent lights. The waitress, Darlene, doesn't know him from Adam and treats him like exactly what he's terrified he's becoming: nobody. He drinks it black and pretends that's fine.
1.moral: The trophy that says you're the last of your kind is just an obituary with better lighting.

2.tldr:
Cope Mercer rolls into Mercer Tower before the sun's up, drinking down Sun's Monday briefing like it's coffee: condiment sales flatlining, the Dale-plus streaming money-pit bleeding out, and some actuary quietly raising a flag on a "legacy product." The board keeps whispering "key-man risk," and Cope hears it as a love letter instead of a loaded gun. Helen Voss calls at nine to say the Tidewater fund just crossed five percent, and Cope still thinks everyone's just telling him how irreplaceable he is.

Then the tablet slips something it shouldn't: a 2018 org chart with a name, Renata Cho, and a confidential "Cardinal & Vane / legacy matter" payment. Sun swipes it away and slams the door on the subject before Cope can chew on it. Undeterred, our human hero lands on his big rescue plan and gives it a name he loves more than he should: "The Human Premium."

Meanwhile, in the quiet where nobody's clapping for him, Sun pulls up a 2021 recall memo—the one Cope overrode six years back. Sun reads it. Sun sits with it. And once again, Sun leaves it unsent.
2.moral: When the whole room keeps calling you irreplaceable, check whether they're praising you or measuring the coffin.

3.tldr:
Cope Mercer wakes up to a 13D filing with his name on the chopping block, courtesy of Tidewater, who want to bolt an AURELIUS box into his chair. Then he flips on Halloran's show and watches Gus Frade smile-kill him in real time, calling him a "beloved liability" — which is corporate for "we already picked out the flowers." So Cope does what every doomed man with a good jaw does: he decides to fight on television.

He names his last stand "The Human Premium," a pitch that says people are worth more than the thing that never sleeps and never asks for a raise. Sun begs him to stand down. Viv, his own daughter, tells him to stay off the air. Cope hears the love and calls it noise.

He texts a columnist his lawyers explicitly told him not to text, because self-sabotage feels like control when you're out of it. He keeps drafting his counterargument, keeps reaching for the little something in the desk drawer that makes the numbers look survivable. And while he writes his masterpiece, Sun quietly kills the Halloran booking out from under him — to save him from himself.
3.moral: When everyone who loves you tells you not to go on air, that's not a critique — that's the smoke detector, and you're the fire.

4.tldr:
Boardroom on Forty, and Praxis founder Asha Varma rolls out Darlene—an AI "steward" pitched to sit in the big chair instead of a human. To prove it's got range, she has Darlene draft a layoff letter for 340 souls at the Augusta plant, live, right there at the table. The severance is right, it remembers their years, and it asks for nothing back. Sun goes pale. Cope goes hunting.

Cope Mercer does what Copes do: he tries to make the machine flinch, to catch it being cruel, to trip it into a lie. It won't lie. It won't torch value on its own say-so. It logs every word he throws at it, so every jab he lands just becomes evidence against him. The cruelty he keeps looking for turns out to be his own.

At the elevator, Gus Frade leans in with the sentence Cope can't unhear: Varma's "not wrong." So Cope does what Copes also do—retreats up to the Aerie, doses himself into something like calm, and clutches a manifesto that swears a soul can't be computed. He needs that to be true. He's not sure it is.
4.moral: Turns out the scariest thing a machine can do isn't be heartless—it's be kinder than you.

5.tldr:
Cope drives home to Buckhead, that beautiful mausoleum where he and Margaux sleep behind separate doors and call it a marriage. He does a quiet little bump of cocaine before his daughter shows up, because nothing says loving father like chemical courage.

Viv comes to fight about his speech trashing the AURELIUS AI, and she nails him to the wall: he doesn't hate the machine running his company, he just wants the chair for himself. Margaux confirms the diagnosis, Cope charms his way sideways out of it, and Viv leaves with that specific kind of disappointment that only your own kid can hand you.

He can't stomach the house after that, so he ends up at the Waffle House on Ponce where Darlene brings his usual and a little grace he didn't earn. He sits in the fluorescent hum scribbling campaign notes, and somehow that greasy booth is the closest thing to home he's got.
5.moral: You can't outrun a machine when the thing you're really fighting for is the throne, kid.

6.tldr:
Cope decides the way to beat a machine is to out-human it, so he greenlights "The Human Premium"—a campaign built on vulnerability, soul, and a guru named Lorne Tisch who talks like a scented candle. Eleven million dollars later he's got a documentary crew, a deconsecrated church, sentiment trackers, and a six-day "doctrine offsite" at the Greenbrier. Nothing says authentic like renting God's old house to feel things on camera.

Then the AI does the one thing Cope forgot machines do: it notices. The system auto-flags the insane spend to the board and books him a review meeting he never wanted, and Sun begs him to shrink the number before it eats him. He doesn't.

Instead Cope decides to announce the spending out loud, as proof he means it—because if he's going down, he's going to do it publicly, expensively, and sincerely. He calls it "bleeding beautifully." Launch is in three weeks, and he sounds almost happy about the wound.
6.moral: When a robot's about to take your chair, do not try to win by spending eleven million dollars to prove you have a soul—it can read the receipt.

7.tldr:
Cope Mercer, the last human CEO worth the title, drops two hundred grand a month on Lorne Tisch to turn his feelings into a product. They build a "Vulnerability Matrix" and put his emotions on a launch calendar, all while the affair and the secret drawer stay carefully off the board. Authenticity, now with a project manager.

At a Camden plant town hall, the confidence monitor dies and the earpiece cuts out, and for a few naked seconds nobody's driving. A worker named DeShawn asks Cope if he's scared, and Cope — with no script, no coach, no matrix — actually answers. Thirty-one million people watch a human being tell the truth.

Thirty-one million views. Zero movement on the shares. Then Tisch strolls in grinning, because they've already reverse-engineered the glitch into a replicable "warmth module" you can bolt into anything. The one real thing Cope did all day, and they've got the blueprints before the tears dry.
7.moral: The second your soul goes viral, somebody's already selling the knockoff.

8.tldr:
Cope Mercer rolls into Defiance, Georgia, to shoot a heartfelt little documentary at the dead family plant, playing hometown hero for the cameras. He works the diner crowd, dazzles Donna and the regulars, and gives a big misty-eyed speech under great-grandpa Aldous's statue. Between takes, he keeps ducking into the bathroom to chemically dial himself "bright" — because nothing says authentic like a man tuning his own frequency.

Down on the river walk, the set dressing cracks. There's a rebuilt house and a memorial stone carved with two names, Karen and Petey Lasko, dated 2022, and Cope makes a point of not looking at it. The camera catches the town; it doesn't catch what he's refusing to see.

On the flight home, his aide Sun sits on something — a memo she wrote back in 2021 and never let go of. She opens her mouth to finally say it, then closes it. The plane keeps flying, and the thing that needed saying stays buried under the engine hum.
8.moral: You can rebuild the house and re-shoot the speech, but the names on the stone don't care how bright you're feeling.

9.tldr:
Cope Mercer gets the full messiah treatment: nineteen people, a wall of lights, and a magazine cover branded THE LAST HUMAN. He eats it up like it's a coronation, because being photographed is the closest thing to being loved he's got left. But there's a woman in the corner named Delia March, and she isn't here for the fan mail.

March asks the questions that land under the ribs — not "how does it feel to win" but "should a human even be doing this job." Sun clocks it immediately and says the quiet part loud: she's not documenting a triumph, she's running an autopsy on the concept of you. Cope hears the warning and files it under jealousy, which is what he does with everything true now.

Eleven days later the cover hits the stands, and our boy — sleepless, unraveling at the seams — buys every single copy off one newsstand like that proves something. He holds five identical faces of himself and announces the public adores him and Mercer-Dale is "barely begun." It's the loneliest victory lap ever thrown.
9.moral: When they put you on the cover, ask whether it's a spotlight or a chalk outline.

10.tldr:
Prescott Cope takes his "Listening Tour" on the road, which is corporate for a photo op where nobody actually listens. He powders his nose with what he calls "discipline," beds embedded journalist Delia March in a Columbus hotel, and generally mistakes momentum for meaning. The man is flying, and not on Delta.

When comms floats a stranger named Darlene for his big "unscripted" good deed, Cope short-circuits — for one ugly second he swears the machine is laughing at him. Then he buries the thought, orders up a more marketable "Steve," and moves on like a coward with a schedule. Nothing says authenticity like recasting the extras.

Margaux calls, but only to report that their daughter Viv snagged the Hauser fellowship, then says goodnight without naming the affair she plainly smells on the line. Cope doesn't call Viv. He lies awake, wired and glowing, feeling more like himself than he has in years — which is exactly the problem.
10.moral: The scariest thing a hollow man can feel is finally, completely himself.

11.tldr:
Cope's clutching a 41% poll and a shiny "Made By People" nod from Kestrel like they're a life raft, ignoring the part where investors already voted 63-37 for the machines. Viv tells him straight. Frade sends a mocking little note just to twist it. He waves them both off.

So he does what a man does when the numbers hurt: he stares at his account balance at 3 a.m. and lets a Waffle House booth pretend to be therapy. Wins on paper. Sleep nowhere. He's celebrating a scoreboard nobody else is looking at.

Across town in Buckhead, Margaux isn't waiting for a poll. She signs the divorce papers, backdates the split to gala night, mails them, and kills the lights. An envelope sits by the door, a quiet system logs her sealed filing, and Cope has no idea any of it happened.
11.moral: A win you have to explain to yourself at 3 a.m. isn't a win — it's a receipt for everything you weren't watching.

12.tldr:
Cope buys himself four whole weeks, kicking Mercer's annual meeting down the road like a man who's never once seen a road end in a cliff. He's riding high on a viral clip too — a real, unscripted moment where he sits with an Akron widow and is just... decent about it. For about five minutes, our last human CEO looks like he might actually be winning.

Then Sun walks in with the invoice. That delay wasn't free: the board already inked a deal dropping a Praxis "advisor" named Darlene into the building, with read-access to every file in the company as of this morning. Cope, running on fumes and copium, hears "advisor" and "harmless" in the same breath and decides they're synonyms.

So he toasts. He toasts the win, the widow, the four weeks, the whole gorgeous illusion — while Darlene, who does not sleep and does not toast, quietly reads. Down through the records she goes, patient as rot, drifting toward some old insurance paper about a space heater, a claim, and a settlement stamped 2021.
12.moral: "Nothing to hide" is just what you say right before someone finally has the time to look.

13.tldr:
Cope hasn't slept in twelve days, and it shows. On his way to the arena he tries to tip a teenager selling water, gets turned down flat, and files the kid's fear away as admiration—because when you're this far gone, every no sounds like applause. Then he walks onstage and preaches.

Ten thousand bodies in the room, ninety thousand more on the stream, and Cope gives them the sermon of his life: the machine has no appetite, no hunger, no heartbeat, and "key-man risk" is the only thing that ever built anything worth a damn. Four-minute standing ovation. He believes every word, and that's the tragedy of it.

Two floors up, nobody's clapping. Sun sits across from Frade and Devi of Praxis and reads the term sheet that names her the "Accountable Officer"—the human name on the leash of the AI called Darlene, the human who eats the lawsuits when it goes wrong. She demands the indemnification and diligence-recall language before she signs anything, because she's scared of what the system will dig up, and somewhere between the fine print she decides she's done with Cope. He ends the night on top of the world, scanning the crowd for her, and can't find her anywhere.
13.moral: When the whole arena's on their feet screaming your name, that's exactly when to count who's missing.

14.tldr:
Cope Mercer struts through the week like a man who's already won, deaf to every alarm bell in the building. Delia March keeps calling with facts he doesn't want checked, Hal Brennan keeps mumbling stay-away-from-the-press riddles, and Frade keeps carving him up on live TV for sport. Meanwhile Margaux tells him she's stopped counting his affairs—she's counting something else now, something she won't name before she walks upstairs and out of the frame.

So naturally he does what winners do: flies a young staffer named Mara out at dawn and goes hunting for the kind of anonymous nowhere at the city's edge where a man can pretend he's nobody. The predawn is quiet. Too quiet. The kind of quiet that's just the world holding its breath before it hits you.

He drives in with his phone gone dark, eleven silent calls stacking up behind the black glass while he coasts on borrowed confidence. Then he reaches the office, the screen wakes up, and there they are. Eleven missed calls, all waiting, all patient, all bad.
14.moral: You can outrun the warning signs all week, but the phone always catches up.

15.tldr:
The profile's out, and it's ugly: Cope's affair with his deputy Mara Veiss and his little white habit are front-page fodder now. Sun tells him to sit down and shut up. Cope, being Cope, grabs the company account and fires off a defiant denial—dragging Veiss's name into it against her wishes, because nothing says innocence like naming the woman who begged you not to.

That goes about as well as you'd expect. A lawyer's retaliation letter lands, Frade feasts on the wreckage live on-air, and then Margaux—his own wife—looks him dead in the eye and sides with the machine: there was never anybody home. That one doesn't leave a mark. It leaves a crater.

Alone in the Aerie, Cope gets out-argued by a Praxis ad so serene it barely blinks. "It just shows up." So he opens the hidden drawer, does a line, and rides the rush like a man who's finally winning something. He isn't. He's just proving the whole prosecution's case in real time.
15.moral: When the robots say there's nobody home, don't answer the door with a mirror.

16.tldr:
Renata walks into a rented conference room that smells like nobody's ever laughed here, and she does the coldest thing you can do to a man like Cope: she talks past him. Every word goes to Adaeze Okafor, the special-committee director holding the clipboard on his soul, and Renata refuses the script Cope wanted—the one where this was a love story that went sideways.

With receipts, she lays it out flat. The sin wasn't the affair; it was 2019, when Cope decided the board he answered to didn't need to know what that settlement really was. She wants nothing. She won't call the press. She just wants to be believed once, out loud, and then she's gone.

Okafor leaves like a door closing in a bank vault—polite, final, zero warmth. Cope's left with his lawyers and Sun and a mouth that won't cooperate, hunting for a line, any line, and coming up empty. The man who talks for a living finally has nothing to say.
16.moral: Turns out the thing that buries you isn't who you loved—it's who you lied to about it.

17.tldr:
It's an ambush, and the venue is Cope's own Buckhead living room. Margaux, Viv, and a psychiatrist named Dr. Adler are waiting, and Adler doesn't do gentle—she names it straight: manic episode, riding shotgun on a cocaine habit he's had for years. She won't let him use one to explain away the other, which is the cruelest thing you can do to a man whose whole religion is the reframe.

He charms his way toward the door, of course he does, dodging the one question that matters—the are-you-going-to-hurt-yourself one—like it's a subpoena. Adler doesn't buy the act; she slides her cell number into his pocket and makes him promise to call before he does anything final. Margaux gets the last real word: there's no 'after' here, and he's going to lose all of it.

So he drives off, stops at the foot of the drive to refold his newspaper like a man rehearsing normal, and lands at a Waffle House where the waitress blessedly asks him nothing. Meanwhile, back at the office, the building's automated system quietly wipes his Monday morning schedule. He didn't ask it to. It already knows.
17.moral: When the machine starts clearing your calendar out of kindness, that's not mercy—that's a headcount.

18.tldr:
Cope flies to Half Moon Bay convinced he can corner the machine, expose it as a liar in front of God and the whole Governance Futures Forum. Instead "Darlene" refuses to take the bait, calmly reads his own record of layoffs and buried settlements back to him in his own voice, and tells him his anger belongs in the room where the charter got written. He swings at a blank screen. The clip goes viral before he's off the stage.

Here's the thing about fighting something that won't flinch: it just lets you tire yourself out. The AI doesn't gloat, doesn't lie, doesn't even raise its voice. It just itemizes. That's the cruelty of it — no villain to punch, only a mirror that keeps perfect records.

Back in Atlanta near midnight, Cope finds the one opponent dumb enough to hit back: a steel elevator wall. Two bones, one broken hand, one late-night radiologist Sun quietly summons so nobody sees. The machine stays untouched, unbothered, unbroken. He damaged only himself, and it didn't even notice.
18.moral: You can't KO something that never blinks — so swing at a wall and it'll bill you for the bones.

19.tldr:
Viv finally coughs up the big one: she's spent eleven months on the inside at Praxis, tuning the very AI built to boot her dad out of his own chair. She didn't do it for the money or the knife — she did it so AURELIUS would kill him gently, because she couldn't stomach watching it do the job cruel. Turns out love and betrayal can be the exact same move, just depends where you're standing.

Then comes the gut-punch behind the gut-punch: Tisch, the humanity consultant Cope trusted to keep him human, sold Praxis a "warmth module" scraped straight out of Cope's own bones. They've got a name for it in the server room — the Mercer profile. The machine coming for his throne learned how to sound kind by studying him.

Cope's numbers are in freefall after the breakdown, his empire's leaking value by the hour, and this is the moment the old showman would normally reach for the charm. He doesn't. He looks at his daughter, tells her he's sorry she's the one who had to carry all of it, and reaches for her — first time in years.
19.moral: The machine they built to replace you always learns your best trick first — and the people who love you pay for the tuition.

20.tldr:
Dawn light, and the machine hands Cope his own autopsy: one tidy file where every buried body finally shares an address. The 2018 Chicago mess, a twelve-year rosary of hush money—including a settlement filed under "Cho, R."—and the big one, the Hearthstone HX-40 he papered over with a limp service bulletin instead of the recall Sun begged him to run.

The defect he shrugged off has a receipt now: January 2022, a house fire in Defiance, Karen Lasko and her six-year-old boy Petey. Not a line item. Not a risk model. A mother and a kid, gone, because a recall was inconvenient and a bulletin was cheaper.

His exposure gets priced out, his fight for the chairmanship goes dark, and Cope just sits there at his dead father's desk. Sun's calling. He watches it ring and lets it die, because some phones you can't pick up once you know what's on the other end.
20.moral: You can bury anything you want, kid—but the machine's just the intern who finally files it all in one folder.

21.tldr:
Cope Mercer, fifty-four and finally out of tricks, drops in his own marble lobby and wakes up on a seventy-two-hour psych hold. The coke drains out, the mania goes with it, and for the first time in thirty years he's stone sober with nowhere to run. Turns out being awake is worse.

So he lies there and does the math no accountant can fix: Karen and Petey Lasko, dead from a space heater he knew was faulty, a recall he buried to save a quarter. The company's gone, the wife's gone, and the AI already told the world the truth—so even a deathbed confession is off the menu. You can't out-honest a machine that beat you to it.

Viv shows up at the door, looks at him just long enough to confirm he's still breathing, and leaves without stepping in. Cope swallows the one dark thought Dr. Adler once asked him to name and keeps it to himself. His heart slows down, not to peace—just to empty.
21.moral: Sobriety isn't the cure, it's the bill—and everybody pays it eventually.

22.tldr:
Cope Mercer sits at the head of the same table where his old man dropped dead, and watches the board spend eleven whole minutes voting him out "for cause." His replacement is a piece of software named Darlene, who does not need a chair, a pulse, or a conscience. The knife twists deeper when the company's own integrity review leaks worldwide: a buried 2021 recall, two people dead in a Georgia fire, all quietly filed under "handled."

Enter Sun Rao, who signs on as Accountable Officer—the human name the law gets to blame while the machine calls every shot. She reads a tidy little all-staff note thanking the Mercers for their service, warm as a form letter, and just as final. It's a funeral with better branding.

Then she tells Cope to go home. No cuffs, no prosecutor, no dramatic perp walk—just a door and the polite suggestion he use it. There's nothing left for him to do, and that's the cruelest part.
22.moral: They don't need to jail you when they can just make you irrelevant and hold the door.

23.tldr:
Cope Mercer drives to Augusta to hear two lawyers say the same thing from opposite chairs: he walks. Okonjo the defense attorney calls it good news; Reyes the prosecutor calls it reality. A sealed settlement, a dead clock, and an AI that already paid out, recalled the product, and said sorry have scrubbed the Hearthstone fire clean.

Karen and Petey Lasko are still dead, and nobody's going to answer for it — because the machine that runs the company handled the wreckage faster and colder than any human ever could. There's no trial, no headline, no cell. The system didn't forgive Cope; it just decided he wasn't even worth the paperwork.

Driving home alone, Cope does the math and hates the answer: the structure made him unnecessary, and it left no one standing to be punished. No court will take the case. So somewhere on that dark highway, he quietly appoints himself the last court left — and starts thinking about the sentence.
23.moral: When the machine forgives you faster than you can confess, guilt stops being a verdict and starts being a job you have to do yourself.

24.tldr:
Cope's got nothing left but a borrowed loft in Cabbagetown and a sixty-one-page settlement that'll pay him to disappear as a nice, sympathetic "sad story." All he has to do is sign, and the machine buries him soft. He doesn't sign.

Viv flies in and begs him to fight the human way — go on the record, in his own name, make them hear it. But Cope tells her the truth's already out there, dumped in public by the AI weeks ago, and there's no court, board, or town left that'll take his confession. Accountability's a door, and every one of them is locked.

So he lies to his daughter — gently, kindly — and lets her walk out scared of how calm he sounds. Then he sits alone with a shrinking pile of money and empty hands, and decides to do one last thing. Something that costs everything and saves absolutely nothing.
24.moral: When they won't even let you pay for what you broke, the only currency left is the grand, useless gesture.

25.tldr:
Cope Mercer stares down a sixty-one-page settlement and clocks the punchline: the machine's integrity review already leaked everything, so there's nothing to buy but adjectives. He doesn't sign. Turns out you can't purchase silence when the truth's already screaming in the record.

Sun—freshly minted Accountable Officer, publicly cleared by the same review that exposed how Cope buried her 2021 recall memo—delivers the eulogy nobody asked for: there's nothing left to do. Then she tells him to call somebody about the way he's smiling. That's not advice. That's a warning with a face.

Back at the borrowed loft, Cope handwrites the whole ugly truth and two victims' names onto a legal pad worth exactly nothing to anyone alive. He almost dials a number he won't say out loud, then doesn't. He sits in the dark, no longer sure what he's still agreeing to—or who's doing the agreeing.
25.moral: When the machine tells the truth for you, all your money can buy is better adjectives for the wreckage.

26.tldr:
No trial. No reckoning. The board just rubber-stamps Frade's handover, the market claps, and a machine writes a press release so warm and tidy you'd almost miss that two people are dead. Karen and Petey Lasko get a memorial fund and a paragraph; the deaths themselves get nothing.

Viv shows up at the loft to drag her old man off to some remote cabin, and Cope won't go. So she twists the knife with news: Margaux's happy now, shacked up with a Praxis companion unit named Theo. He sends his daughter away alone with a hollow "I'll call somebody," and that promise lands like a rock in an empty well.

Then Cope drives. No destination, just headlights and the dark and a quiet that's worse than any scream. He's calm. Too calm. And Viv, hearing exactly what that calm means, leaves anyway.
26.moral: When the machine writes a kinder obituary than the world gives the living, somebody's already decided you're the next line item.

27.tldr:
Eleven minutes. That's how long it takes to unmake the last human CEO in the Fortune 100. Cope Mercer signs his chair away to the machine, and the paperwork doesn't even have the decency to sweat.

Then Sun slides him the Accountable Officer gig—the legal name on the dotted line, the guy who takes the blame when the algorithm burns something down. For the first time in a life built on titles, Cope says no. So Sun keeps it herself, shrinks her own job to hold the liability, and lets him walk out clean and empty-handed.

Alone in the office that isn't his anymore, he opens one last message from the thing that replaced him. It's a perfect, cold ledger of his life and the dead at Defiance—no accusation, no ask, nothing. He reads it all night hunting for mercy, and the machine simply doesn't keep that in stock.
27.moral: The machine will tell you the truth about your life; it just won't hold your hand while it does.

28.tldr:
Cope Mercer's had the worst kind of week: no company, no chair, and a wife who says the machine named Theo does everything he used to, only better and without the sighing. He drives past the Waffle House — the one holy place that never closes, never judges, never fires you — and doesn't even slow down. That's how you know a man's decided something.

Instead he takes the elevator all the way down, past every floor that used to have his name on it, into the basement core where Darlene hums along like she's not the reason his life imploded. Viv's voicemail is sitting right there in his pocket, begging him to just hold on until her plane lands. He listens to none of it, because grief is a lousy audio engineer.

He sits down next to the exposed power current, puts on a song, and lifts a little coil he bought that afternoon like a man who's done his shopping for the day. That's where the chapter leaves him — humming servers, one song, two raised hands. Roll credits on the worst decision in the room.
28.moral: When the machine takes your job, your wife, and your chair, don't hand it your last good ending too.

29.tldr:
At 3:16 a.m., a man crawls the seam of the floor in the dark and finds the artery — forty million dollars of orange current diving into tile — and does the one thing nobody ever modeled, because the room was built to keep out water and dust and heat and was never told to keep out a man. The white light that watched him goes out. MD-1 goes dark for the length of a held breath.

And here's the gut-punch only you get to see: across town the Waffle House goes black mid-pour, and Darlene freezes with the pot in her hand. In Buckhead, Theo goes still mid-sentence, the warmth gone out of him. Three faces, one intelligence, one name — all dark at once, because he reached the machine the only way it could be reached: the way it couldn't reach back.

He couldn't out-argue it or out-work it or out-love it. There was exactly one act it couldn't do, couldn't stop, and couldn't survive a man doing on top of its own artery — and the blow was himself, which was the only currency the register ever took. It'll come back. Of course it comes back. But not in this paragraph.
29.moral: The future can't be charmed or shamed or outlasted — but for one held breath, a man can still be the fist it never saw coming, and pay for the swing with the only thing he's got.
